messages.say:
I’m pulling the common governance and enforcement pieces across your files and reducing them to reusable building blocks that can span projects.

Thinking through file citation process


I'm evaluating file citations. The results from a tool search don't show the required line numbers, but the file search normally gives those. Maybe I should search for exact phrases to get the full context with line numbers. But if those aren't available, I might have to reconsider how I cite.


Clarifying citation and explaining components


It looks like the tool instructions want line ranges, but my snippet results only provide markers. I think I can use those markers for citations. As for the answer, I'll explain common components like policy registries, schema validators, and enforcement kernels. I'll note how project-specific aspects, like business rules, still apply.


Thought for 1m 25s

Yes.


Most governance/enforcement frameworks keep reinventing the same control spine. Your files already point to that. The reusable part is not the business logic. It is the enforcement kernel around it.


Your own gate design says the same thing plainly: only a small adapter changes per gate, while the runner, self-healing loop, schemas, and result shapes stay shared. In that model, GateSpec is project-specific data, but execution, evidence validation, failure normalization, and remediation orchestration are reusable.


01260207201000000474_Gate‑Speci…


The common pieces that repeat across frameworks are these:


Rule source / policy registry
One authoritative place for patterns, invariants, and allowed behavior. Your guardrails system uses PATTERN_INDEX.yaml as the authority and SYSTEM_INVARIANTS.md as the rules layer.

Validation gates
Pre-execution and post-execution gates are universal. Pattern exists, schema valid, scope allowed, evidence produced, protected paths untouched, anti-patterns clear. That shows up in both your guardrails docs and invariants.

Contracts and schemas
Structured inputs, outputs, evidence, and result objects are reusable across projects. Your gate files explicitly separate shared schemas like gate_spec.schema.json, gate_result.schema.json, and heal_sequence_report.schema.json from gate-specific content.
01260207201000000474_Gate‑Speci…

Shared runner/orchestrator
The logic that runs checks, validates evidence, normalizes failures, and emits results is usually reusable. Your files already describe gate_runner.py, self_heal_orchestrator.py, and error_normalizer.py as shared modules.
01260207201000000474_Gate‑Speci…

Failure taxonomy + recovery library
Most serious systems end up with canonical failure modes, recovery mappings, and reusable heal templates. Your invariants require recovery paths, and the gate docs centralize failure modes and heal templates instead of duplicating retry logic per gate.

Scope and safety controls
Path scope, protected paths, execution limits, timeouts, concurrency rules, and manual review triggers are reusable enforcement primitives. Your invariants define exactly those categories.
SYSTEM_INVARIANTS

Audit and evidence layer
Logs, result records, evidence bundles, and metrics are almost always reusable if their shapes are standardized. Your files require audit trails, consistent GateResult shapes, and metrics/reporting driven off shared outputs.

Compile-time and CI enforcement
The strongest frameworks do not rely on people behaving. They wire checks into planning, pre-run validation, commit-time hooks, and CI. Your comparative analysis calls this out as a major governance commonality across systems.
Comparative Analysis of DDC Sys…


The practical rule is this:


Make the enforcement engine reusable. Make the project-specific variation declarative.


That means the reusable layer should own:


rule evaluation

gate execution

schema validation

failure normalization

audit logging

recovery orchestration

protected-path enforcement

metrics collection


The project layer should only supply:


pattern registry entries

invariants/policies

gate specs

evidence schemas unique to that domain

failure mode additions

path scopes and limits

workflow dependencies


That split is already reflected in your material. The technical spec pushes pattern-first execution, pipeline boundary contracts, traceability, and validation as framework-level capabilities, not one-off project hacks.


NEWPHASEPLANPROCESS_TECHNICAL_S…


Bluntly: the reusable components are the ones that answer how enforcement works.
The non-reusable components are the ones that answer what this specific project must enforce.


A clean cross-project architecture would look like this:


Reusable enforcement kernel


pattern registry loader

invariant engine

gate runner

schema validator

self-heal orchestrator

anti-pattern detector

audit writer

metrics reporter

dependency/DAG executor


Project pack


PATTERN_INDEX

invariant file

gate specs

failure mode config

path protection list

project schemas

project-specific manual-review rules


That is the real commonality across frameworks: same skeleton, different policy pack.


One caution: your current stack is already strong on configuration-driven governance of work, but not always on configuration-driven architecture of the resulting implementation. That means the enforcement layer is reusable now, but if you want deeper reuse, you need to force more behavior into pattern/config plus registered executors instead of custom imperative logic.


ChatGPT-Configuration-Driven De…


So the answer is not just “yes.” It is:


Yes, and your files already contain the blueprint for it.
The reusable unit is a governance/enforcement platform with project-specific policy packs layered on top.


I can turn this into a concrete “core framework vs project extension” file inventory next.

I’m checking the files that directly address configuration-driven development, pattern governance, and reusable gate components so the answer is tied to your framework instead of generic theory.

Considering line numbers for citation


I’m thinking that the file still doesn't show line numbers, which is needed for citations. I might try using search with exact phrases, as this could return results with lines, and then I can cite those. I’ll focus on getting the right lines for accurate citations.


Considering citation approach without line numbers


I’m wondering if it’s impossible to use lines for citations since the tool doesn't show them. Maybe I can still use a citation without line numbers, like filecite, although the system prefers exact lines. Still, I could just cite the whole file or mention the source directly to stay concise.


Thought for 36s

Yes. Governance and enforcement are one of the best places to use that model.


Your gate system already looks like an incomplete version of it: the gate-specific part is mostly data, while the runner, healing loop, schemas, error normalization, dependency handling, and metrics stay shared. The files say GateSpec is the only gate-specific configuration, and everything else about execution, evidence validation, result emission, and healing is handled by shared components. They also say that when you add or modify a gate, you should change data, not code.


01260207201000000474_Gate‑Speci…


01260207201000000474_Gate‑Speci…


So the answer is not just “can it.” It is:


It should.


The right split is:


Static engine layer


gate runner

self-heal orchestrator

error normalizer

schema validator

dependency resolver

audit writer

metrics/report generator

protected-path and scope enforcement

lock/concurrency handling


External specification layer


pattern registry

gate specs

failure modes

heal templates

dependency graph

path scopes

runtime limits

evidence contracts

config schemas

policy/invariant files


That matches your current invariants too. The system already expects pattern IDs, schema validation, observable evidence, non-bypassable guardrails, protected paths, anti-pattern detection, audit logging, and automatic pre/post validation gates. Those are exactly the kinds of things a generic enforcement engine should read from config instead of hardcoding per project.


SYSTEM_INVARIANTS


SYSTEM_INVARIANTS


Where you are already close:


Your current template stack already enforces configuration-driven governance of work: plan-as-contract, pattern-first execution, fail-closed guardrails, and no ad hoc execution inputs. But your own evaluation says it does not yet force full configuration-driven development in the stronger sense where a stable engine performs work and external spec/config fully determines behavior. Right now it still allows behavior to leak into custom executor code, and it still leaves a loophole when no pattern exists.


ChatGPT-Configuration-Driven De…


ChatGPT-Configuration-Driven De…


That means the reusable governance components can absolutely be designed in this style, but you need to harden the model from:


pattern-aware
to
executor-bound, spec-driven, fail-closed


The missing pieces are the same ones already identified in your files:


every executable step must bind to a registered executor plus validated spec/config

no pattern means create and validate the pattern first

no null pattern_id on executable steps

prose or prompt text cannot act as hidden runtime behavior

spec-only definitions without engine binding are invalid
ChatGPT-Configuration-Driven De…
ChatGPT-Configuration-Driven De…


So the clean architecture is:


1. Universal enforcement engine
A stable package with commands like:


run-gate

validate-pattern

apply-guardrails

run-recovery

write-audit

check-scope


2. Project policy packs
Each project supplies:


PATTERN_INDEX.yaml

SYSTEM_INVARIANTS.md or structured equivalent

gates/*.spec.json

configs/failure_modes.json

heal_templates/*.yaml

schemas/*.json

gate_dependencies.json


3. Executor registry
A formal registry of allowed executors and what schemas/patterns they are allowed to consume.


4. Mandatory validation
Execution only consumes:


approved plan JSON

registered pattern specs

schema-valid config instances
and nothing else. Your own proposed hardening language already points in exactly that direction.
ChatGPT-Configuration-Driven De…


One important caution:


Do not stop at “data-driven gate configs.” That is the weak version.


The stronger version you want is:


code = generic engine

config/spec = behavior surface

schemas = legality boundary

evidence = proof surface

registry = authorization surface


Without that, you get the failure mode your files already warn about: a spec exists, but there is no real engine behind it, or the real behavior still lives in bespoke code.


ChatGPT-Configuration-Driven De…


So the blunt answer is:


Yes, and governance/enforcement is probably the best domain to apply Configuration-Driven Development first.
It fits naturally because governance logic is mostly reusable control logic plus project-specific rules. Your current system is already halfway there.


The next move is to define a formal Governance Enforcement Engine and a project policy pack schema.

I’m mapping the full workflow and separating where CDD is a good fit from where it would be overkill or dangerous.

Considering citation issue with line markers


I need to finalize the answer concisely, but citations require line markers. The results from mclick don’t include those markers. I could try using filecite, but that lacks line references too. Maybe I should run a more targeted search to get the necessary line markers for citations.


Deciding on citation format for response


I still don’t have line markers, and while I could use snippet markers, the system requires exact lines. Some previous responses used filecite without lines, though. I could try the snippet marker approach but it feels risky. I’ll aim for a concise, detailed response and mention where CDD should and shouldn’t be used. No need for a web search here.


Defining CDD usage categories


I’ll break this down into categories for clarity:


Heavily used: intake boundary objects, converters, pattern assignment, workstream definitions, validator catalogs, CLI contracts, error handling, orchestration profiles, deterministic context, evidence generation, and GitHub mapping.

Used selectively: generated code archetypes, infrastructure profiles.

Not primary: core business logic, unique algorithms, exception handling requiring judgment.


The conclusion: CDD is essential almost everywhere except for specific domain algorithms.


Thought for 36s

Yes. Looking at the whole process, CDD should be used in far more places than just governance and enforcement.


The blunt answer is:


Use CDD for every layer that is primarily about transformation, validation, routing, orchestration, policy, and reproducibility.
Do not force it onto the narrow slice that is genuinely novel domain logic.


Your own files already show the gap clearly: the system is configuration-driven at the planning and execution-control layer, but not yet at the implementation-architecture layer.


ChatGPT-Configuration-Driven De…


Here is where CDD should be used across the full process.


1. Idea intake and planning boundaries


This is one of the strongest fits.


Your planning pipeline already has a formal boundary object flow: CCIS → Phase Plan → Workstream JSON → Multi-Agent Execution, with schemas, validators, converters, and a final package acting as the sole execution input. It also already names core services like ConfigManager, SchemaRegistry, and DeterministicContext. That is exactly where CDD belongs.


CDD should control:


idea intake card / CCIS schema

requirements normalization

pattern assignment

workstream generation

phase DAG generation

final execution package generation


Meaning: these should all be produced by stable converters reading validated schemas and config, not by ad hoc prompt behavior.


2. OpenSpec → planning → GitHub decomposition


Your workflow explicitly starts with OpenSpec/user input, then Claude Code project management creates the plan, parallel workstreams, and GitHub project decomposition.


Planning file


This whole section should be CDD-driven:


spec templates

issue decomposition rules

workstream splitting rules

GitHub field mappings

milestone/epic/sub-issue generation

dependency and conflict policies


This should not depend on “how the model feels today.” It should run from explicit decomposition configs and mapping schemas.


3. Prompt transformation layer


This is another strong candidate.


Your workflow includes a distinct “Codex Prompt transformation” stage that converts workstreams into Aider-compatible prompts.


Planning file


That should be driven by:


prompt schemas

task-kind-to-prompt-template mappings

allowed variable slots

model/tool capability profiles

output contract expectations


In other words, prompt generation should be an engine plus templates/parameters, not handcrafted every time.


4. Pattern selection and executor binding


This is where you most obviously need more CDD than you have now.


Your own CDD evaluation says the system already has pattern-first scaffolding, but still does not force the stronger rule that executable behavior must be driven by registered executors consuming validated spec/config. It also says missing patterns should trigger pattern creation before implementation, instead of allowing freehand implementation to proceed.


So CDD should be used for:


pattern registry

executor registry

task-kind → pattern mappings

pattern package definitions

config schema validation

behavior-binding proof


This is the control point that turns “pattern-aware” into real configuration-driven implementation.


5. File mutation planning and workstream splitting


Your process already describes a .json modification package that gets parsed into independent workstreams across create, edit, and remove operations.


Planning file


That whole area should be CDD-driven:


mutation classification

file conflict rules

workstream independence criteria

merge eligibility

path/domain routing

worktree allocation


This should be a generic engine consuming mutation manifests, not custom logic per feature.


6. Validation and error pipelines


This is one of the best CDD targets in the whole system.


Your workflow already has a named error pipeline with validators, linters, secret scanners, tests, and follow-on correction. Your planning docs also show repeated validation gates, reference integrity gates, DAG gates, scope validation, and replayable deterministic runs.


Use CDD for:


validator catalogs

toolchain profiles by language/domain

gate sequences

severity thresholds

autofix eligibility

escalation rules

retry budgets

evidence requirements


This is exactly where a stable engine plus config is strongest.


7. Runtime verification and environment execution


You specifically call out “unknown runtime verification” as a missing stage in the workflow.


Planning file


That should absolutely be CDD-driven:


environment profiles

VM/container execution recipes

test datasets / fixtures

timeout and retry rules

artifact capture rules

rollback conditions

pass/fail criteria


That lets you swap local machine, VM, or container targets without rewriting the execution engine.


8. Audit, evidence, and reporting


This should be heavily configuration-driven.


Your files emphasize trace storage, event capture, replay, validation evidence, and audit trails. They also explicitly warn against fabricated artifacts and require real evidence generation.


CDD should govern:


evidence bundle schemas

audit event shapes

report generation rules

metrics formulas

normalization rules

trace retention policies


Reports and evidence are classic “engine + schema + policy” territory.


9. CLI contracts and developer-facing tool behavior


Yes, here too.


Your error-prevention guide explicitly says CLI examples must be complete, CLI behavior must match documented spec, and weak modes must be clearly separated from governance-grade modes.


AI Implementation Plan Error Pr…


That means CDD should be used for:


CLI command definitions

argument schemas

help-text validation

mode policies

default behavior policies

doc-example execution tests


Your CLI layer should be thin wrappers over stable engines, driven by command specs.


10. Generated code archetypes


This is a selective but important fit.


Your planning file already describes file archetype patterns like CLI entrypoint, adapter, pipeline step, config/schema definition, and test module. It frames patterns as “shape + rules” with parameters and slots.


Planning file


CDD should be used for:


CLI wrappers

adapters

pipeline steps

config/schema files

test skeletons

orchestration scripts


This is where template engines and pattern packages pay off.


11. What should not be forced into CDD


Do not try to make everything configuration-driven.


Do not force CDD as the primary model for:


novel domain algorithms

genuinely new business logic

one-off exploratory prototypes

tricky optimization logic

cases where the behavior cannot be captured cleanly in schemas/config without hiding complexity


Your own CDD evaluation already makes the distinction: the system is strong on configuration-driven governance of work, but weaker on configuration-driven architecture of the resulting implementation. That means the right model is not “everything becomes config.” It is “everything repeatable becomes config-driven, and the truly custom part stays in bounded extension slots.”


The practical rule


Across your entire process, CDD should be the default for these five classes of things:


boundary objects

transformations

orchestration/routing

validation/recovery

evidence/reporting


And it should be optional or constrained for:


deep domain logic

novel algorithms

explicitly marked extension slots


So the strongest expansion areas are:


planning intake, decomposition, prompt transformation, mutation/workstream generation, validator pipelines, runtime verification, audit/evidence, CLI contracts, and code archetype generation.


That is where you will get the biggest reduction in drift, rework, and freehand AI behavior.


One sentence version:
Use CDD everywhere the job is to move, validate, route, or prove information; don’t use it as a blunt instrument for the small part of the system that is genuinely new logic.
